section.title: Shimei Curses David
section.sectionRationale: As David flees, he meets Ziba with provisions and a slander against Mephibosheth, then Shimei, a man of Saul's house, who pelts him with stones and curses, calling him a man of blood. David refuses to let Abishai kill Shimei, submitting to the possibility that the LORD is behind the cursing and hoping God may repay him with good. Meanwhile Absalom enters Jerusalem, publicly takes David's concubines on Ahithophel's advice, fulfilling Nathan's word. The movement shows David humbled under reproach and God's judgment unfolding, even as David's shrewd counter-plan quietly proceeds.
section.thesis: In his lowest hour David absorbs a bitter cursing without retaliation, submitting to God's hand even in Shimei's abuse and hoping for God's mercy, while Absalom's public seizure of the royal concubines fulfills the very judgment Nathan announced, so that humility under reproach and the outworking of God's word stand side by side.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Ziba's provisions and slander against Mephibosheth, verses 1 to 4; Shimei's cursing and David's humble restraint, verses 5 to 14; and Absalom in Jerusalem taking the concubines on Ahithophel's counsel, verses 15 to 23.

verses.text: When David had gone a little beyond the summit, Ziba the servant of Mephibosheth was there to meet him. He had a pair of saddled donkeys loaded with two hundred loaves of bread, a hundred clusters of raisins, a hundred summer fruits, and a skin of wine.

“Why do you have these?” asked the king. Ziba replied, “The donkeys are for the king’s household to ride, the bread and summer fruit are for the young men to eat, and the wine is to refresh those who become exhausted in the wilderness.”

“Where is your master’s grandson?” asked the king. And Ziba answered, “Indeed, he is staying in Jerusalem, for he has said, ‘Today, the house of Israel will restore to me the kingdom of my grandfather.’”
So the king said to Ziba, “All that belongs to Mephibosheth is now yours!” “I humbly bow before you,” said Ziba. “May I find favor in your eyes, my lord the king!”

units.label: Shimei curses David
units.note: let him curse, the LORD has told him

verses.text: As King David approached Bahurim, a man from the family of the house of Saul was just coming out. His name was Shimei son of Gera, and as he approached, he kept yelling out curses.
He threw stones at David and at all the servants of the king, though the troops and all the mighty men were on David’s right and left.
And as he yelled {{qalal|curses}}, Shimei said, “Get out, get out, you worthless man of bloodshed!

The LORD has paid you back for all the blood of the house of Saul, in whose place you have reigned, and the LORD has delivered the kingdom into the hand of your son Absalom. See, you have come to ruin because you are a man of bloodshed!”
Then Abishai son of Zeruiah said to the king, “Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? Let me go over and cut off his head!”
But the king replied, “What have I to do with you, O sons of Zeruiah? If he curses me because the LORD told him, ‘Curse David,’ who can ask, ‘Why did you do this?’”

Then David said to Abishai and to all his servants, “Behold, my own son, my own flesh and blood, seeks my life. How much more, then, this Benjamite! Leave him alone and let him curse me, for the LORD has told him so.
Perhaps the LORD will see my {{oni|affliction}} and repay me with good for the cursing I receive today.”
So David and his men proceeded along the road as Shimei went along the ridge of the hill opposite him. As Shimei went, he yelled curses, threw stones, and flung dust at David.
Finally, the king and all the people with him arrived, exhausted. And there he refreshed himself.

units.label: Absalom takes the concubines
units.note: in the sight of all Israel

verses.text: Then Absalom and all the men of Israel came to Jerusalem, and Ahithophel was with him.
And David’s friend Hushai the Archite went to Absalom and said to him, “Long live the king! Long live the king!”
“Is this the loyalty you show your friend?” Absalom replied. “Why did you not go with your friend?”
“Not at all,” Hushai answered. “For the one chosen by the LORD, by this people, and by all the men of Israel, his I will be, and with him I will remain.
Furthermore, whom should I serve if not his son? As I served in your father’s presence, so also I will serve in yours.”
Then Absalom said to Ahithophel, “Give me counsel. What should we do?”

Ahithophel replied, “Sleep with your father’s concubines, whom he has left to take care of the palace. When all Israel hears that you have become a stench to your father, then the hands of all who are with you will be strengthened.”
So they pitched a tent for Absalom on the roof, and he slept with his father’s concubines in the sight of all Israel.
Now in those days the advice of Ahithophel was like the consultation of the word of God. Such was the regard that both David and Absalom had for Ahithophel’s advice.

qalal.gloss: To curse, to treat as light, trifling, or of no account, to revile. Shimei relentlessly qalal, curses, David, calling him a man of blood and declaring his fall God's judgment. To curse the LORD's anointed was a grave offense, yet David bears it, wondering whether the LORD Himself stands behind the reviling and might turn it to good.

oni.gloss: Affliction, misery, humiliation, the suffering of the lowly and oppressed. David hopes the LORD will look on his oni, his affliction, and repay him with good for the cursing. The word ties David's humbled state to the God who sees the affliction of His people, as He saw Israel's in Egypt, and answers it with mercy.

basic.context: David's flight continues, and along the way he meets two men from the house of Saul who reveal much about his humbled state. First is Ziba, Mephibosheth's servant, who brings generous provisions and then slanders his master, claiming Mephibosheth has stayed in Jerusalem hoping to reclaim Saul's throne. David, with no time to investigate, hastily grants Ziba all of Mephibosheth's property, a judgment he will have to revisit later. Then comes Shimei, a kinsman of Saul, who walks along cursing David, hurling stones and dust, and calling him a worthless man of blood who is now reaping God's judgment for the house of Saul. Abishai wants to behead him, but David restrains him with remarkable humility: perhaps the LORD has told Shimei to curse, and perhaps God will see David's affliction and repay him with good. So David absorbs the abuse and presses on, exhausted. Meanwhile Absalom enters Jerusalem in triumph. Hushai, David's secret agent, feigns loyalty to Absalom. And on Ahithophel's advice, Absalom publicly takes David's ten concubines on the palace roof, a brazen claim to the throne that fulfills, almost word for word, Nathan's judgment that David's wives would be taken in broad daylight. The chapter raises questions about bearing reproach humbly, discerning God's hand in suffering, and the sober unfolding of God's announced judgment.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that interweaves David's humility with the outworking of God's judgment. David's restraint of Abishai models a striking submission to God's providence even in unjust abuse, though his hasty verdict on Mephibosheth also shows he is not infallible. The statement that the LORD 'told' Shimei to curse should be read within David's humble uncertainty ('if,' 'perhaps'), affirming God's overarching sovereignty without excusing Shimei's malice, which David later still judges. The public violation of the concubines is reported as the fulfillment of Nathan's word and the fruit of Ahithophel's wicked counsel, not endorsed.
basic.originalLanguages: Shimei relentlessly qalal, curses, David, treating the LORD's anointed as worthless, a grave act that David nonetheless refuses to avenge. Instead David hopes that the LORD will look on his oni, his affliction, the humiliation of the lowly, and repay him with good. The two words frame David's posture: reviled and pelted, he neither retaliates nor despairs, but entrusts his humbled state to the God who sees affliction and answers it with mercy.
basic.typology: David, the anointed king unjustly reviled as a man of blood, bearing curses and stones without retaliation and entrusting himself to God, foreshadows Christ, who was reviled and reviled not in return, who bore false accusation in silence, and who committed Himself to the Father who judges justly. David's hope that God would see his affliction and repay good anticipates the vindication God gave His suffering Son.
basic.christ: David reviled yet not retaliating points directly to Christ. When Shimei curses and stones him, David forbids vengeance and hopes for God's mercy; when Christ was cursed, mocked, and struck, He did not retaliate but entrusted Himself to the Father, and God repaid His affliction with the good of resurrection and exaltation. David is falsely called a man of blood; Christ, truly innocent, was numbered with transgressors. The suffering king who bears reproach and trusts God is fulfilled in the suffering Servant.
basic.god: God is the one David sees behind even Shimei's cursing, so that David bows to God's providence rather than lashing out, trusting that the LORD who ordains his affliction may yet repay him with good. God is also the one whose word through Nathan is now being fulfilled in Absalom's public sin, showing that God's announced judgments come to pass. Thus God is revealed as sovereign over both the abuse David suffers and the consequences of David's own past sin, and as the God who sees affliction and answers it.
basic.crossReferences: Second Samuel 12 records Nathan's prophecy, now fulfilled, that David's wives would be taken in broad daylight. First Peter 2 presents Christ, who when reviled did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to God. Second Samuel 19 tells how David later spares and then, through Solomon, judges Shimei. And Romans 12 counsels leaving vengeance to God, as David does here.
basic.oneStory: At his lowest, the anointed king bears cursing and reproach without retaliation, entrusting his affliction to God, while God's word of judgment is fulfilled through Absalom's brazen sin. This humbled, reviled king who will not avenge himself but hopes in God points forward to Jesus, the truly innocent King who bore reproach in silence and was vindicated by the Father who saw His affliction.

context.literary: The chapter pairs two encounters on David's road of humiliation, Ziba and Shimei, with the scene of Absalom's triumph in Jerusalem, contrasting the humbled true king with the exalted usurper. Shimei's cursing echoes the theme of David as a man caught in the consequences of bloodshed, while David's restraint recalls his earlier refusals to harm Saul. Absalom's taking of the concubines deliberately fulfills Nathan's words from chapter 12, tying the rebellion back to David's own sin.
context.historical: Publicly taking a king's harem was a recognized way of claiming his throne, which is why Ahithophel's counsel would rally the rebels; it was a point of no return that committed Absalom fully. Shimei's cursing reflects lingering resentment among Saul's clan, the Benjamites, over David's rise. Bahurim was a Benjamite village on the route east from Jerusalem. Ahithophel's counsel being esteemed 'like the word of God' shows his extraordinary reputation, which makes his defeat in the next chapter all the more clearly God's doing.
context.canonical: The chapter fulfills the specific judgment of 2 Samuel 12:11 to 12 and continues the passion-like humiliation of the rejected king begun in chapter 15. David's bearing of reproach becomes, in Christian reading, a foreshadowing of Christ's silent endurance of reviling, which 1 Peter draws out. The concubines' violation and its fulfillment of prophecy underscore the Bible's theme that God's word, whether promise or judgment, unfailingly comes to pass.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To portray David humbled under reproach, bearing Shimei's curses without vengeance and entrusting his affliction to God, while showing God's announced judgment fulfilled through Absalom's public sin, so that the reader sees both a model of humble submission and the sober certainty of God's word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes David's restraint, his hasty judgment on Mephibosheth, and Absalom's wickedness, commending David's humility while not endorsing his rash verdict or Absalom's sin. What endures is the call to bear reproach without retaliation, entrusting vindication to God, the danger of acting on one-sided reports, and the certainty that God's word of judgment, like His word of promise, is fulfilled.

debates.question: In what sense had the LORD 'told' Shimei to curse David?

views.label: God's overruling sovereignty, not a command excusing Shimei
views.case: David speaks carefully, 'if the LORD told him,' and 'perhaps the LORD will repay me with good,' expressing humble submission to God's providence rather than a claim that God authored Shimei's malice; God sovereignly ordains that David face this reproach as part of his humbling and discipline, yet Shimei remains morally responsible, which is why David can still later regard his cursing as a real wrong, 1 Kings 2:8-9, so God's sovereignty and Shimei's guilt stand together.

views.label: David's faith reading God's discipline into his suffering
views.case: Others stress that David, chastened by his own sin, receives the cursing as part of God's just discipline and refuses to fight it, interpreting even an enemy's abuse as something God can use for his good; on this view David is not making a metaphysical claim about God commanding Shimei but humbly submitting to God's hand in the affliction, hoping for mercy.

debates.bearing: Both agree David bows to God's sovereign hand in his suffering without excusing Shimei's sin; the passage models humble submission to God's providence in the face of unjust reproach, entrusting vindication to the LORD who sees affliction, a posture perfected in Christ who when reviled did not revile in return.

study.originalLanguages: The scene on the road turns on the verb qalal, to curse, to treat as worthless: Shimei relentlessly curses David, hurling both stones and the vilest words, branding the LORD's anointed a man of blood. To curse the anointed king was a capital-worthy offense, and Abishai is ready to answer it with the sword, yet David restrains him and bears the reviling. Instead of vengeance, David reaches for another word, oni, affliction, the humiliation of the lowly and oppressed: 'Perhaps the LORD will see my affliction and repay me with good for the cursing I receive today,' verse 12. The same word describes Israel's affliction in Egypt that God saw and answered. So David's posture is framed by these two words. Cursed and pelted, he neither retaliates nor collapses, but entrusts his humbled, afflicted state to the God who looks upon the affliction of His people and answers it with mercy, a trust that reaches its perfection in Christ, who bore reviling in silence and was vindicated by the Father.

typology.type: The anointed king reviled and stoned, refusing to retaliate
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who when reviled did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to God who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.

typology.type: The king falsely branded a man of blood
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the innocent One numbered with transgressors and falsely accused, Isaiah 53:12 and Mark 15:28.

typology.type: Hope that God will see the affliction and repay with good
typology.fulfillment: Christ's affliction answered by the Father with resurrection and exaltation, Philippians 2:8-9.

study.christ: David reviled yet not retaliating points directly to Christ. When Shimei curses and stones him, David forbids Abishai's vengeance and hopes only that the LORD will see his affliction and repay him with good; when Christ was cursed, mocked, spat upon, and struck, He did not retaliate but entrusted Himself to the Father who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23, and God repaid His affliction with the good of resurrection and exaltation, Philippians 2:8-9. David is falsely called a man of blood, though the reproach touches his real guilt; Christ, wholly innocent, was numbered with transgressors and bore reproach that was not His own, Isaiah 53:12. The humbled, reviled king who will not avenge himself but hopes in God is fulfilled in the suffering Servant who bore reviling in silence and was vindicated by His Father.
study.god: God is the one David discerns even behind Shimei's cursing, so that he bows to God's sovereign providence rather than lashing out, trusting that the LORD who has ordained his affliction may yet look on it and repay him with good. God is also the one whose word through Nathan is now being fulfilled, as Absalom publicly takes the concubines in the very manner God had foretold, showing that God's announced judgments come to pass as surely as His promises. Thus God is revealed as sovereign over both the reproach David suffers and the consequences of David's own sin, and as the God who sees the affliction of the lowly and answers it, so that even in humiliation David's hope in Him is not misplaced.

crossReferences.note: Nathan's prophecy that David's wives would be taken in broad daylight before all Israel, fulfilled by Absalom on the palace roof.
Christ, when reviled, did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to God who judges justly, the pattern David foreshadows under Shimei's curses.
Leave room for God's wrath, for vengeance is His; David's refusal to let Abishai kill Shimei embodies this trust.
David later remembers Shimei's cursing as a real wrong, showing that submitting to God's providence did not erase Shimei's guilt.

study.oneStory: At his lowest, the anointed king bears cursing, stones, and reproach without retaliation, entrusting his affliction to the God who sees, while God's word of judgment through Nathan is fulfilled in Absalom's brazen sin. This humbled, reviled king who will not avenge himself but hopes in God's mercy points forward to Jesus, the truly innocent King who bore reproach in silence, refused to revile in return, and was vindicated by the Father who saw His affliction and raised Him from the dead. The road of the suffering king runs from Bahurim to Golgotha and on to glory.

quiz.q: How does David respond when Shimei curses him and throws stones?

options.text: He forbids Abishai from killing Shimei, submitting to the possibility that the LORD is behind it and hoping God will repay him with good.
options.note: Verses 10-12 show David's humble restraint and trust rather than retaliation.

options.text: He orders his soldiers to seize and execute Shimei on the spot.
options.note: The opposite; David restrains Abishai from harming Shimei.

options.text: He restrains his men and bears the cursing without revenge.
options.note: True, but this option omits David's striking hope that the LORD may see his affliction and repay him with good.

options.text: He ignores Shimei entirely and says nothing about God's role.
options.note: David does speak, discerning God's hand in the cursing and hoping for His mercy, not merely ignoring it.

quiz.q: What does Absalom do on Ahithophel's advice, and what does it fulfill?

options.text: He publicly takes David's concubines on the palace roof, fulfilling Nathan's word that David's wives would be taken in broad daylight.
options.note: Verses 21-22 fulfill the judgment of 2 Samuel 12:11-12.

options.text: He simply moves into the palace and takes up residence quietly.
options.note: The act is a deliberate, public claim to the throne, not a quiet move, and it fulfills specific prophecy.

options.text: He seizes David's harem to claim the throne, as Ahithophel counseled.
options.note: True, but this option omits that it fulfills Nathan's specific prophecy of judgment on David.

options.text: He destroys the palace to erase David's memory.
options.note: He takes the concubines to claim the throne; he does not destroy the palace.

quiz.q: What does David hope for as he endures Shimei's cursing?

options.text: That the LORD will see his affliction and repay him with good for the cursing he receives.
options.note: Verse 12 records David's hope in God's mercy amid his humiliation.

options.text: That his soldiers will remember the insult and avenge it later.
options.note: David entrusts the matter to God, not to future vengeance by his men.

options.text: That God will notice his suffering and be gracious to him.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific hope that God would repay good for the cursing itself.

options.text: That Shimei will repent and stop cursing him.
options.note: David's hope is in God's response to his affliction, not primarily in Shimei's repentance.

quiz.q: Why is David's hasty grant of Mephibosheth's property to Ziba noteworthy?

options.text: David acts on Ziba's one-sided slander without investigating, a rash judgment he will later have to revisit.
options.note: Verses 3-4 show David believing Ziba's accusation without hearing Mephibosheth, revisited in chapter 19.

options.text: It proves that David was always fair and never made mistakes in judgment.
options.note: The opposite; this is a rash, one-sided verdict, showing David is not infallible.

options.text: David believes Ziba's report too quickly and rules against Mephibosheth unheard.
options.note: True, but this option omits that David later has to reconsider the matter when he hears the other side.

options.text: David refuses Ziba's gift and reserves judgment until he returns.
options.note: David accepts the gift and grants Ziba the property immediately, rather than reserving judgment.

quiz.q: How does David's endurance of reproach point to Christ?

options.text: Reviled and not retaliating, entrusting his affliction to God, David foreshadows Christ who when reviled did not revile in return but trusted the Father.
options.note: David's restraint prefigures Christ, 1 Peter 2:23.

options.text: It teaches that God's king should always crush those who insult him.
options.note: David does the opposite, refusing vengeance, and Christ bore reviling in silence.

options.text: It models bearing insult without revenge, leaving vindication to God.
options.note: True, but this option omits the direct foreshadowing of Christ who committed Himself to the Father who judges justly.

options.text: It shows that suffering is always a sign of God's rejection.
options.note: David hopes God will repay good for his affliction; suffering here is discipline and testing, not rejection, and Christ's suffering led to glory.

questions: David bore Shimei's curses without striking back, wondering if God was even in it. When you are insulted or slandered, how do you respond, and could you entrust the matter to God instead of retaliating?
David hoped the LORD would see his affliction and repay him with good. Where in a current hardship do you need to hope that God sees and will bring good out of it?
David acted rashly on Ziba's one-sided report against Mephibosheth. Where are you at risk of judging a situation or a person on incomplete or slanted information?
Absalom's public sin fulfilled the exact judgment Nathan had spoken years earlier. How does the certainty that God's word comes to pass, in both warning and promise, shape the way you live now?
Christ bore reviling in silence and was vindicated by the Father. How does His example strengthen you to endure unfair treatment without bitterness or revenge?

translationNotes.note: David's words 'if the LORD told him, Curse David' express humble submission to God's sovereign providence in his suffering, not a claim that God authored Shimei's malice; David still later regards the cursing as a real wrong, 1 Kings 2:8-9.
The BSB footnote notes that some Septuagint manuscripts read the king 'arrived at the Jordan' exhausted, specifying the destination of this stage of David's flight.

Absalom's public taking of David's concubines on the roof deliberately fulfills Nathan's prophecy in 2 Samuel 12:11-12; the act is reported as the outworking of judgment and Ahithophel's wicked counsel, not as approved conduct.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 16:1-23. An em dash in the source at verse 18 was converted to a comma; no dashes remain in this file.
